data.title: Retail Sales Rise 0.6% In February, More Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Advance Retail Sales Report from the Census Bureau showed consumer spending was more robust than expected in February. Headline sales rose 0.6%, a step up from January's 0.1% decline and better than the projected 0.5% growth.

For an inflation-adjusted perspective on retail sales, take a look at our Real Retail Sales commentary.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance Estimates of U.S. Retail and Food Services

Advance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for February 2026, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $738.4 billion, up 0.6 percent (±0.4 percent) from the previous month, and up 3.7 percent (±0.5 percent) from February 2025. Total sales for the December 2025 through February 2026 period were up 3.1 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The December 2025 to January 2026 percent change was revised from down 0.2 percent (±0.4 percent)* to down 0.1 percent (±0.3 percent)*.

Retail trade sales were up 0.6 percent (±0.5 percent) from January 2026, and up 3.5 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 7.5 percent (±1.6 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 5.2 percent (±1.8 percent) from February 2025.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 3.7% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) rose 0.5% in February, up from January's 0.0% reading and higher than the expected 0.3% growth.

Core retail sales are up 3.6% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline and building materials as well as food services & drinking places. The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases rose 0.5% in February. This is up from January's 0.2% reading and was higher than the expected 0.3% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 3.8% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rigetti Computing: Some Progress, But Still Overhyped
data.description:
Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI) is one of the several pure-play public companies focused on capturing the vastly expanding quantum technology market. This is an area that is largely untapped and one where investors are willing to pay high premiums to own a small piece of potential winners.

With an ever-evolving narrative, there has been multiple opportunities to buy/sell stocks across the quantum landscape. However, there still appears to be a significant amount of hype embedded into shares. My last report on RGTI was at the end of January, where I argued shares reflected a high probability of success in an otherwise uncertain environment.

Rigetti's approach to quantum is based on superconducting qubits, which are designed, built, and fabricated all by Rigetti. In quantum, the technological approach is truly a competitive differentiator. Companies such as Rigetti, D-Wave (QBTS), Quantum Computing (QUBT), and IonQ (IONQ) are all taking different approaches to achieve quantum computing advancements. In my opinion, it has yet to be seen which method will garner the most traction and be the most effective, which is why volatility across this sector exists.

Since then, the stock has fallen over 45%, a material underperformance. However, I don't think the stock has yet to find a bottom. The recent quarterly update was a positive step forward, but there is still a long ways - and much uncertainty - until "success" can be achieved.

With many emerging technological trends, with Quantum and AI being great real-time examples, investors often overlook how quickly near-term success can be achieved. This often results in aggressive premiums paid to own the perceived winning stocks. However in reality, this can take multiple years to play out.

Of course, there is a lot of money to be made if invested in the early winners, but with quantum, I believe visibility into who those winners will be is still multiple quarters away.

For now, I believe investors are better waiting until the path to success is more clearly defined.

Operational Updates

For the vast majority of stocks, the most important part of a quarterly earnings is the financial update. With quantum - an emerging technologies in general - the operational advancements and competitive positioning are magnitudes more important.

During the most recent quarter, RGTI made several important announcements that demonstrated ongoing technological advancements. The most important one, in my opinion, was the continued fidelity improvements across monolithic and chiplet architectures.

Rigetti recently achieved a two-qubit gate fidelity as high as 99.9% at 28 nanosecond gate speed on a prototype platform using its new proprietary adiabatic CZ scheme. The Company continues to be at 99.9% one-qubit gate fidelity and has also reported median two-qubit gate fidelities of 99.7% on its 9-qubit system, 99.6% on its 36-qubit system, and 99% on its 108-qubit system (Cepheus-1-108Q). Together, these milestones reflect sustained progress in materials, fabrication, and system-level design and further narrow the fidelity gap between superconducting systems and other quantum modalities, while continuing to be about 1,000 times faster than some modalities like trapped ion or pure atoms.

While a bit technical in nature, this advancement is clear progress towards the company's long-term outlook, as shown below. It is highly unlikely that these achievements happen exactly according to plan, but investors will continue to applaud the progress.

More importantly, the company provided some insight into its expected technological advancements over the next two years. With these targets now established, investors will surely look for ongoing execution. RGTI has needed to delay some timelines in the past, though typically only by a quarter or so.

Looking ahead, 2026 is about execution and scaling. Our near-term priority is completing deployment of the 108-qubit system at 99.5% median 2-qubit gate fidelity, which we expect around the end of March. Beyond that, our focus is to deploy a system with more than 150-qubits with an anticipated 99.7% median 2-qubit gate fidelity around end of December 2026. As far as we know, no one has demonstrated systems at that scale and fidelity using chiplet based architecture.

In parallel, we'll continue advancing our chiplet architecture as the foundation for scaling toward a system of more than 1,000 qubits with an anticipated 99.8% median 2-qubit gate fidelity by or around the end of 2027. Chiplets are central to our strategy and represent the most practical path to large-scale systems.

Achieving the above targets would be considered a giant success relative to where the current technology stands. However, this will also put more weight on RGTI's ability to actually achieve them in the stated timeline. I tend to be a little skeptical with product timelines as unforeseen challenges frequently arise. Add on the fact that competition across the space is increasing, especially with larger players such as Google and IBM, and this could cast a sentiment headwind for the next few quarters.

Just last week, RGTI announced plans to invest $100 million into the UK to accelerate quantum computing development. This is a material investment relative to RGTI's size (more on that below) and is a clear indication that the company foresees demand building. The goal of this investment is to build and deploy a quantum computer with 1,000 qubits in the next 3-4 years. Again, this is a multi-year target that may require additional funding and an uncertain outcome. That is not to say RGTI won't be successful, but there continues to be a lot of risk factors at play.

Financial Update

From a financial standpoint, the company ended the year with $1.9 million in revenue and an operating loss of $22.6 million. Given the early-stage nature of this opportunity, this dynamic is largely expected by investors over the next several quarters, if not years.

Two important factors to consider.

First, RGTI ended the quarter with $589.8 million in cash/investments, giving them enough runway to support the $100 million investment into the UK as well as funding operations for the foreseeable future. FCF burn during 2025 was ~$77 million. This is extremely important because the business is more than likely to run at an operating loss for several more years. If cash burn accelerates, this could eventually cause a strain on RGTI's liquidity, which would result in the company needing to raise debt/equity.

Second, RGTI announced updates to several contracts. This includes an $8.4 million purchase order from India's Centre for Development of Advanced Computing, and ongoing progress towards the $5.7 million contracts previously announced with Novera. At the end of March, RGTI also announced it sold a 9-qubit Novera QPU to the University of Saskatchewan. While relatively small contracts compared to the company's operating losses, this demonstrates small steps forward.

At the end of the day, liquidity and balance sheet strength are key to success. Rigetti has a strong balance sheet and enough liquidity to keep investing.

Valuation

As seen with the company's financial standing, it becomes very challenging to place a revenue multiple with broad-based commercialization still a few years away. It goes without saying, but revenue is essential for a company to succeed. Right now, investors are paying a high premium to own success in a company that may come several years from now.

Valuations across the main quantum companies are still sky-high. Does this eliminate the chances of the stock working? Of course not. But it may place a cap for how high of a premium investors are willing to pay.

Right now, RGTI stands out as an outlier to the high-side of valuation. With the company's revenue still well below that of its competitors, RGTI seems to be the earliest-stage investment opportunity, which comes with added risk.

Risks

The obvious risk comes in the form of technological challenges. If RGTI is not able to prove out its quantum methodology, the stock will not work. This takes a vast amount of resources - both financial and operational - which could strain on the company's liquidity position. Any delays in product advancements would be detrimental.

Additionally, increased competition from Google and IBM will likely weigh on the broader sector. Both of these company's have much greater financial resources and technological capabilities. This does not guarantee their success, but it surely puts them at an advantage. In addition, if the revenue trajectory starts to push out even further, Google and IBM have the resources to acquire the best technology available, which would increase the competitive threats.

Final Thoughts

Although I remain bullish on the long-term opportunity for quantum, I believe RGTI's stock is pricing in a very high probability of success relative to its current standing.

The company's technological roadmap is impressive, but this multi-year outlook gives investors a lot of time to find a better entry point. The volatility across the sector is unlikely to subside anytime soon, which supports my belief that waiting patiently on the sidelines is the best course of action for now.

data.title: Broadcom: The Moat Still Holds
data.description:
Taking advantage of the recent earnings release of Broadcom (AVGO), I have decided to review how the integration of VMware from more than two years ago is performing in the numbers. It is a relevant moment due to the market situation, and because I think it also makes sense to check the health of the company’s moat before buying any internal or external narrative around the stock.

Before going into the numbers, my first impression after reviewing the price has been, are we in a distribution phase?, could it be the prelude to a decline phase?. As a starter, I would say that the current multiple does not leave much room if the situation starts to deteriorate, but also that the management narrative arguing strength can be demonstrated. Let’s see then in what situation we find ourselves.

The moat is real

We start from the results obtained by AVGO, that is, from the $19.3B of total revenue, of which the Semiconductor Solutions segment represents 65%. This is relevant before continuing because just one FY ago, the weight was 58%/42%, and the main reason this is happening is AI.

AI revenue continues accelerating, and they already add eleven consecutive quarters, since in FY23 it amounted to $3.8B, in FY24 $12.2B, in FY25 $19.9B, and only in this Q1 of FY26, it already stands at $8.4B. For me, this is structural if we take into account that a change is taking place in the conception of computing infrastructure in hyperscalers, that is, with an XPU specifically designed for the workloads of these companies, it translates into more predictable memory access patterns, fixed network topologies, and basically, models that, in addition to being executed millions of times, end up being between 40% and 65% cheaper in TCO at gigawatt scale.

And I say structural because AVGO co-designs these XPUs with its clients, translating the architecture into manufacturable silicon with TSMC (TSMC), which has the positive aspect of not assuming manufacturing risk.

With a range of $600B and $700B of CapEx from hyperscalers, of which 75% will be allocated to this AI infrastructure, together with the visibility that AVGO already has of more than $100B in AI chips for 2027, for me it is sufficient proof that if a negative event occurs, it will come from the hyperscalers themselves in terms of growth moderation rather than from AVGO’s competitive advantage.

Another important part regarding the segment is to go a bit deeper into the composition in relation to AI revenue. I start with the TPU program of Google (GOOG), which in my view is the main engine, with a notable evolution of Ironwood. Why do I consider it relevant? Because for me, each TPU generation that Google deploys can be translated as more orders, thus confirming that the dependency relationship continues expanding.

Additionally, we have recently had a public confirmation from Anthropic pointing out its intention to access up to one million Ironwood chips for its Claude models, so here we have GOOG starting to sell capacity directly to third parties. And we already know what that means for AVGO.

Meta Platforms (META), for its part, also confirms that it has hundreds of thousands of MTIA chips running in production for Facebook and Instagram, which it is co-developing with AVGO. With an approximate roadmap of four new versions in two years, we have another of the big players very linked to the company in a structural design process, which from my point of view is very difficult to replace easily.

Now, it is also fair to mention the less attractive part, since not all hyperscalers depend on the company, as Amazon Web Services designs its Trainium chips with Marvell and AlChip, and Microsoft (MSFT) does the same with Maia. Therefore, the only most relevant active XPU customers end up being Google, Meta, Anthropic and OpenAI, thus showing a concentration in AI revenue, with Google at the top.

This is important to mention as I consider it a relevant risk in the case that 40% or 50% of the segment’s revenue decides to diversify towards other suppliers or move to internalise the design, we would have an impact on the financial model that would leave the current valuation as simply disproportionate.

Exceptional margins, with an eroding base

We now arrive at the second segment, where I think it can be said that the post-VMware transition is being executed by management as expected. We have that the software gross margin already reaches 93%, with an operating margin of more than 77%, and a segment generating $27B this past FY25.

In this latest report, the segment has generated $6.8B and has had a growth of 1.4% YoY, a figure I want to highlight because for me it contrasts with the 26% growth rate of the segment in the full FY25. If the operating margin is maintained, the RPO exceeds $45B, and the bookings of the quarter have exceeded $9.2B, what is the reason behind this growth?

In my opinion, this is happening because before a client could buy a component, and use it indefinitely, but with the new business model of VMware, now an annual subscription must be paid for a full bundle that sometimes includes functionalities that the client does not need, and that ends up affecting the bill since its calculation is no longer per servers.

That is, if a company with an infrastructure like current ones where a server can have between 128 and 192 cores, now the cost assumed by the AVGO client is based on these cores, so the result is a multiplication in the company’s revenue without having added additional functionalities.

For this reason, I think that this “forced” cycle is practically completed and that the growth we have seen this FY25 in the segment has largely been the accounting capture of this effect. So it is a possibility that this FY we will see that growth at this rate is the current reality of the business.

In addition, at this cruising speed, we must add an erosion in the revenue base, since for example Nutanix reported 2,700 migrations of VMware clients in FY25, Red Hat accumulates around $500M in OpenShift Virtualization contracts in two years, not to mention that Gartner predicts that 70% of enterprise clients will migrate 50% of their workloads by 2028.

So despite the fact that the clients who remain are paying more, as the 19% increase in ARR shows, the monetization base is gradually contracting. Faced with this, management justifies itself by arguing that VCF will act as an indispensable layer of AI, that is, that as companies scale AI workloads, they will need more VMware, thus offsetting churn.

I understand this argument, but my view is that there is not enough evidence beyond the technical integrations of VCF with NVIDIA (NVDA) and AMD (AMD), but for now, no hyperscaler or AI frontier company has publicly commented on the use of VCF as the main infrastructure for their training clusters.

Which leads me to deduce that the real market has to be in enterprise inference in private cloud, and to a lesser extent in frontier clusters.

Segment

Revenue

% of Total

Operating Income

Operating Margin

Semiconductor Solutions

$12,515M

65%

$7,503M

59.9%

Infrastructure Software

$6,796M

35%

$5,323M

78.3%

Total

$19,311M

100%

$12,826M

66.4%

What is my conclusion then? That although Infrastructure Software represents 35%, the margin of the segment makes it one of the main levers when it comes to sustaining the profitability of the business. So if the documented churn accelerates even more than what I have outlined, we are in a situation that for me would leave AVGO without a main growth catalyst. Is it possible that this combination is what the market is pricing in? Or on the contrary, is it simply a temporary correction of a growing trend?

The technical structure reflects the risk

What the technical structure of the price tells me in the case of Broadcom is that it is pricing in some type of risk given the trend that has started since mid-December, where the sequence of highs and lows has been decreasing. On a weekly level, the MA200 begins to flatten, something that can already be a warning signal regarding the primary underlying trend.

The problem for me is more in the short term when looking at the daily chart, since the MA30 is acting as a dynamic resistance, with a slope that pushes the price down more and more, and in addition to this, a very important trading volume is being lost, the largest of the last 15 months between $320 and $350. This can cause a zone of trapped sellers to form that press any rebound that occurs in order to obtain liquidity.

So at the price level, for me $277 seems to be the most probable direction that is forming unless there is a change in trend. This would be something normal within a market that would continue to be bullish in the background. The problem would come if that level is lost with volume, since then we would be facing a scenario where the most relevant trading zone would be at a price of around $250.

For now, caution is the best strategy when being out of the stock, and to hold in case of being in.

My Takeaway

With all the data I have collected, I think that the short-term situation for AVGO is Hold. My conclusion is that the moat still stands, and AI revenue continues to be healthy,

One of the “buts” would be the concentration in Google, since I think it can be a risk in the event of a slowdown scenario in the industry, but it is not something for which there is evidence at present, and there is also no evidence that Google is going to reduce its dependency on AVGO, so it is something more related to the sector’s cyclical situation that needs to be addressed rather than this specific relationship.

And my other “but” would be that Infrastructure Software generates very good margins, and that is the reason which, at the same time, means that despite having a 35% weight in the revenue mix, if the churn described in the article continues accelerating, the impact would be greater than what the weight in sales suggests. My hypothesis is that these reasons are what the price trend is reflecting, and what are leading to this bearish structure that is amplified due to the current market context, and that may be leading to a higher exposure to liquidity by selling stocks that come from a winning run, in case a greater deterioration occurs.

For now, patience, let someone else earn the first dollar, and we as investors, wait patiently for a positive trend to get into the stock.

data.title: Munich RE: I Expect A Longer Slump In 2026 From New Focus
data.description:
As much as I somehow enjoy showing when I have done something that's slightly off in terms of valuing a business (i see it as a good thing and a good opportunity to learn), I of course like it even more when I get to take a victory lap. Munich RE, which has now underperformed for over a year since 2 articles ago when I rated it "HOLD", as well as my last article on the company, which you can find here, is also a "HOLD".

This goes beyond just a "lucky guess", in my view, and goes to an expertise that I believe I have established to some extent when it comes to looking at Insurance and reinsurance companies, particularly in the European sector. I might not always get the timing 100% right; in fact, I sold both MURGY and ALIZY, two of my main picks years ago, a bit too early. However, since, I have been correct in rating them both "HOLD" due to the state of the industry, macro, and more importantly, their respective valuation. I don't mention the company's fundamentals because there is very little that could be argued to be wrong with MURGY's fundamentals. The company is so superbly rated that it's one of the very few companies in the world with an AA credit rating.

The company, as it stands, has a market capitalization of about 68B EUR. There's also, if you're so inclined, a pretty strong case to make for why things are going to grow from here on out. The reason I'm updating here after about 9 months is that it's time to revisit my share price estimate. While I do believe I was right in "HOLD", it's now time to see if the company deserves an upgrade based on the latest results and estimates.

So without further ado, let's see what we've got going on here.

Munich RE - or Münchner Rückversicherungsgesellschaft is a strong company with superb fundamentals

Do not get the wrong idea because I put the company at "HOLD". Some investors believe that when I do this, I don't believe the company will grow further. This is not in fact the case. The company may well grow further, and one of the primary risks I forecasted in my last article was in fact further outperformance and further growth. However, for some reason, the market has recognized that things have gone "too far".

So despite an annual 2025A growth in AEPS of double digits, pushing the company's earnings to more than €47/share, the company has been on an anaemic trajectory for over a year at the time of writing this article.

What about those results could justify this? After all, these results were higher even than I expected - and as a result of them, I will have to increase my fair value and PT for the company later on.

Looking strictly at the results, there's very little explanation in the company not going significantly higher. The company has overdelivered in every single KPI, and I do not state this lightly. EPS growth is up, DPS growth is up, Solvency is at record levels, close to 300% (we're talking Solvency II, compared to the US RBC ratios), and ROE is close to record levels too - over 200 bps over the company's 2025 ambition. The company's diversified business model, despite my being uncertain about its development, has been delivering excellent earnings.

While the P&C reinsurance by far continues to be the biggest contributor, the company's dividend is now almost completely covered by the ERGO segments, GSI, and L&H reinsurance. For context, MURGY used to be mostly a P&C reinsurer - so being able to do this with comparatively minor segments is an achievement in itself.

To find the reasons for the company's lack of superb performance, to go along with its superb results, we need to dig deeper. Looking at the January and 2026 renewals, here is where we start seeing some signs of issues.

These issues may seem minor, but they may also represent the first stage of a "break." Renewal price changes were negative for the first time in a very long time. What this means is that MURGY was unable to push its pricing due to increased competition for RE pricing. The same was true for volume trends in P&C reinsurance. This is worrying because this is, as mentioned, the company's main industry. Also, the global speciality insurance revenues declined by €200M. I give the company a pass for this because it's clear from the combined ratio (down almost 9%, lower is better), that this was a move of quality and underwriting excellence, which is a net positive.

You will, however, note that the declines are showing up in L&H Re as well. Which means that with only a meager €200M growth in ERGO, the company is basically net negative, or at least not very positive for the year in terms of growth. Yes, every financial KPI is "flying", and the company had a very good 2025 - but there are signals that growth is now starting to become much harder.

So what then gives with this forecast? The expectation is for over 7.6% average annualized growth. How is the company going to achieve this without pricing or volume growth, which it seems is getting harder?

There are a few things that I haven't been happy with the company doing. I don't like that Munich RE has sold what it viewed as "lower-return" businesses. The problem is, many of these so-called "lower return" businesses and contracts were reliable cash cows. And by focusing on higher-margin businesses, the company is essentially conveying a willingness to take higher risks for better returns. This is not something I want out of the company. Munich RE has cited interest in increasing its CSM (contractual service margin) by taking on longevity annuity businesses and large life portfolio transactions. I have been covering portfolios like these for some time - you may remember them.

Because it was exactly this type of risk that caused both Unum (UNM) and Lincoln National (LNC) to "crash". One has recovered, the other not yet. Both were profitable investments - but I wouldn't want to buy the reinsurance company taking on those risks at a premium, that's for sure.

There are more signs of this. The company is targeting specific growth from NA and Asia, with its GSI segment. None of this is low risk.

In essence, Munich RE is conveying again and again that in order to capture the targeted future high margins, it's willing to take risks. I as an analyst, would counter this by saying 'that is fine, but I'll have to "dock" your valuation for that risk'.

It seems evident to me that the company believes its finances are able to handle the higher risk it's taking on - that might be so. It may be this which allows the company to achieve another year of double-digit or 7-8% growth, now that the legacy business is no longer delivering this. The underperformance in P&C insurance surprised me, actually. While it was a deliberate decision to reduce business, going negative was not what I expected. At the same time, some fairly serious NatCats also impacted the company's results - Cali Wildfires and other US events- and now the company seems willing to go more into this.

Bulls would argue that this recent decline is "self-made" by the company, and wait for it to recover once the growth materializes. I say that growth may materialize, but what sort of risk profile will that growth have?

Valuation for Munich RE - Unattractive unless premiumized, which I am still unwilling to do.

I will have to increase my PT here - the company's earnings are too high for me to maintain €400/share. But at the same time, while I am maintaining my PT, I am downgrading the company's overtime premium to reflect its riskier business mix and its ambition to focus on higher-margin plays. It goes without saying that when you are getting higher margins, you have to take higher risks.

So that 12x P/E average, we can forget it.

I believe the company can maintain a 5-7% growth with its new business focus. I further believe that the company will be able to maintain its dividend and to grow it in accordance with plans.

But we need to now introduce the potential for far more volatility, more in line with the 20% miss chance, with a 10-20% margin of error. I'm also going to introduce a more accurate average P/E multiple for reinsurers, which comes to 8-11x. Munich RE is at 12, so I'm going to 10.5x. Before you say that this is too low, consider the company's historical volatility pre-COVID-19, and consider that this may happen again.

Forecasted at the historically accurate 12x P/E (for the previous state of operations), the company would be forecasted to 2028E generate 14.8% annualized RoR with dividend included - below my hurdle, but close to it. However, because of its risk profile, this 10-40% premium to other reinsurers is no longer warranted. Lowered to 10.5x, this ROR comes to less than 5.5% per year without dividends, and only 9% with dividends included. This includes a PT of about €480/share - but this is as high as I go. At €480/share, if bought below, and considering a 10.5x forward P/E, the company is likely to generate annualized returns in excess of 15% per year.

To give you an example of what can happen in case Munich RE is unable to weigh up NatCat losses with releases from reserve or prior year claim reserves, we can look at 2010-2020. 2017 was a major year with major losses from several hurricanes. In 2011, a single quarter loss was enough to turn the company 1Q entirely negative. Munich RE often, during those times, abandons its profit targets, causing the stock price to settle around the more conservative 7-9x P/E we've seen for long periods of time.

As it stands for 2026, I am therefore rating the company a "HOLD" still, thereby echoing most analysts, that, while seeing solid KPI's, see a problematic valuation. I would further make this more problematic by conveying the clear increase in risk the company is taking here.

For MURGY, my new target of €480/share comes to $11.12/share.

Thesis

Munich Re is the largest reinsurance company in the world, and also one of the most conservative in existence. It has a double-A credit rating and a set of fundamentals and titanium-clad underwriting processes. The earnings results for 2025 were good. Due to strategic changes, however, I am marking the company down and using another forecast multiple to reflect its increased risk and shedding of business it considers to be lower-margin.

While some may see a continued positive upside, I see a risk overhang from insurance and the ERGO sector, as well as a general climate risk, proven in historical results, and unaccounted for in risks, in my view.

I therefore give the company a PT of €480/share and a "HOLD" rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €480/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Starwood Property Trust: Discounted Yield With Contained Credit Risk
data.description:
Starwood Property Trust (STWD) is a hybrid commercial mREIT primarily driven by real estate credit (and less so by spreads or corporate lending). So the story becomes less reliant on rate spreads and becomes heavily dependent on asset deployment and credit outcomes. The stock is now trading at a discount to its book value and still yields above 11% in dividends. Most recent concerns surrounding its macro backdrop are of prolonged inflation (oil shock led) that keeps rates higher for longer, and more importantly tests credit quality.

We are already talking about stagflation which delays real estate recovery, in addition to non-accruals pressure and caps overall earnings potential. The most recent quarterly data do not show need for caution right away though. Dividends are well-covered by distributable earnings. There are no widespread credit issues being reported either. Funding conditions also look robust with reasonably low leverage and ample liquidity available.

Overall, I see multiple drivers that could support a Buy (not requiring macro tailwinds) at these discounted levels - new business segments like infrastructure and long term leases are already adding steady cash flow. In fact, if the credit situation deteriorates, Starwood can offset some of the downside with higher servicing income. That is, even though this is not the perfect macro setup for Starwood, the discount assumes worse outcomes than is indicated currently. And Starwood does not need a lot of macro tailwinds either at this point, so any normalization will only boost both a rerating and earnings growth scenario.

Valuations and Implications

At ~0.93x book value, Starwood is not trading at historical lows (like ~0.8x in mid-2023), but this is the highest discount seen since 2024. The ~0.8x valuations were reserved for an environment when markets were fearing a full blown real estate credit crisis, uncertain asset values and earnings. Can that happen again? That can't be ruled out depending on how bad the stagflation scenario shapes up to be. But it will definitely require significantly higher non-accruals than we have seen in the most recent data, liquidity issues hit Commercial Real Estate (CRE) transactions and eventually the dividend coverage worsens. Of course, Starwood's leverage is more conservative today at ~2.4x and liquidity is much better too - so further valuation compression will need panic developments beyond what is indicated today.

What has potentially driven the recent sell-off and valuation compression is a sustained period of sticky inflation and higher for longer rate environment, exacerbated by an uncertain global energy shock. Adding slowing growth to that mix is what makes the stagflation thesis doing the rounds (a very valid thesis). While Starwood does not directly rely on rate spreads, higher for longer rates do present a credit stress threat, and a slower growth environment means repayments will be tested and CRE demand will also be subdued.

What Data Shows

Normalized distributable income (including the effects of undeployed or excess cash and removing one-off losses and timing issues) were reported as ~$1.95, covering the dividend rate of ~$1.92. That also means coverage is thin and depends on further capital deployment, non-accruals trajectory and resolution and hence stable credit conditions.

On the capital deployment front, ~$1.9b commitments are in the pipeline. This is after a strong deployment phase in 2025 where ~$12.7b was deployed, supporting a strong origination environment despite a weak CRE backdrop. As the ~$1.9b commitments pipeline gets funded over the year, we can expect around $0.14 annual EPS drag removed, which was attributed to capital raise timing issues in 2025. That makes the dividend coverage more robust.

The non-accruals picture is not extremely comfortable, but looks manageable. The non-accruals are reported at around $1b and there is over $600m in foreclosed assets reported in Q4 2025. There is a provision of ~$680m to balance out risks. Plus issues seem to be concentrated in specific assets (not systemic). There is evidence to suggest proactive resolution of non-earning assets too. There is around $200m of office loans that have been reported as repaid at par and another ~$200m in process. Management also mentioned multiple multi family assets sold in line with carrying values. That implies resolutions are on track and management is continuing to actively address non-accruals. In fact, every dollar freed up can be re-deployed and support further EPS accretion.

Additional Earnings Drivers

Beyond traditional CRE lending, Starwood has several drivers of earnings growth, some of which could offset a drag in the core earnings in unfavorable scenarios. The net lease segment (~$2.2b) has around full occupancy, with leases spanning over 17 years on average and with built in ~2.3% rent increases annually. The strong setup shows that Starwood could add more to earnings as the segment scales over time. Another relatively new segment - the infrastructure lending segment (~$2.9b with ~$2.6b deployed in 2025) is also expected to generate high returns.

The servicing business has already reported a 47% YoY increase in income (~$107m) - and importantly adds downside protection as this segment is likely to grow in distressed credit conditions (when core income drops). Overall, while the earnings cover dividends by a thin margin today, several structural factors (from resolutions of non-accruals to capital deployment to additional new vectors of growth) should keep earnings supported.

A Buy with Points to Monitor

A key reason to not expect earnings to collapse to 2022-23 levels (and valuations too) is that the earlier stress period was marked by rising short term rates and reliance on repo financing for mREITs. That risk looks contained today looking at Starwood's balance sheet (debt to undepreciated equity leverage of ~2.4x, despite increased leverage from Q3, and ~$1.4b liquidity). Starwood has access to diversified funding sources (including unsecured debt and CLOs) and borrowing spreads are at historical lows. That reduces sensitivity to short term rate volatility and limits chances of forced sales.

The debt to equity ratio chart below also supports this narrative. While it has trended up in the recent quarters, it has not been significantly higher than past historical highs, and leverage is significantly lower than peers (as per management commentary in the Q4 earnings call). Some of the leverage expansion is explained by capital deployment and expansion too. Additionally, the cash and short term investments chart also shows the relative position of strength compared to 2022-23 - they have trended higher into 2025 (~$650-$700m), and are a part of a broader liquidity position of ~$1.4b including available capacity. This is important as liquidity here reflects not just cash, but the ability to fund commitments and absorb stress without forced selling - a key difference from the 2022–23 environment.

Overall, I think Starwood is a decent Buy after recent corrections and despite stagflation risks. A combination of servicing income hedge against credit deterioration environments and low leverage preventing forced selling, positions it better than peers in stress events. Credit risks are currently seen as localized and being worked upon (with evidence to show resolution).

Importantly, even if dividends contract by say 35% to ~$1.25, while the P/B valuations compress to ~0.75x, and the book value declines by ~15% to $16.5, the implied share price correction of ~$4.8 from current levels can be cushioned by less than 4 years of income - which is great for a company exposed to credit risk. On the other hand, a more realistic scenario of valuations reverting to ~1.1x P/B on credit normalization at even current book values could see up to 25% price upside in addition to double digit income.

This Buy call is conditional on monitoring macro and intrinsic variables spiraling into negative territory. The most important variable to watch is the ~$1b non-accruals pool - that should remain contained and not expand or show systemic stress across the portfolio. Non-accruals resolution progress and capital deployment are secondary variables I will watch as they will support earnings, keeping dividend coverage intact.

data.title: Oil Drives South Korean Inflation Higher, But Government Curbs Keep It Contained
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Government measures helped to absorb price shocks partly

South Korea’s consumer price inflation rose 2.2% year-on-year in March (vs. 2.0% in February, 2.3% market consensus). On a monthly basis, prices rose 0.3%, below the market consensus of 0.6%. Rising global oil prices explained most of the increase, though the impact was smaller than expected. Government measures such as the fuel price cap and food vouchers helped to reduce the impact on consumers. Transportation prices rose the most, by 5% YoY, compared to the previous month’s 1.1%. But food prices declined to 0.5% from the previous month’s 2.1%. The March figures indicate that the uptick in commodity prices has not yet broadened to other products or services. Excluding food and energy, core inflation edged down to 2.2% (vs. 2.3% in February, 2.1% market consensus).

Although today’s inflation increase came in below expectations, we expect the recent rise in energy prices to exert a stronger influence in the months ahead. Fuel costs continued to rise despite the price cap. We also expect currency impacts to feed through to domestic prices in the coming months.

Fuel price cap measures helped to limit the gasoline price hike

BoK watch

Price pressures remain relatively contained due to government support, even as domestic demand is poised to weaken. Thus, the Bank of Korea is expected to keep its policy rate at 2.5% at the April meeting. The BoK will likely take a wait-and-see approach as it evaluates whether external shocks are contained or intensify. It will also consider the impact on growth and financial stability.

BoK will take a wait-and-see stance at its April meeting

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Market Brief: The Most Crowded Fear Trade Since 2022
data.description:
The CNN Fear & Greed Index hit 8 on Mar 31, its lowest since November and deep in "Extreme Fear" territory.

The VIX touched 35.3 earlier this month, more than double its year-start level near 14. The S&P 500 sealed its fifth consecutive weekly decline on Friday, the longest losing streak since 2022, and sits roughly 9% below its February peak near 6,900.

By every sentiment metric, this is a market bracing for catastrophe. The price action says otherwise.

The Volatility Gap

The most revealing signal in today's market is not the VIX level itself. It is the gap beneath it.

The S&P 500's 30-day implied volatility has surged above 23%, nearly double where it started the year. Realized volatility over the same window remains below 14%. That roughly 9% spread is one of the widest in recent years.

This gap, known as the volatility risk premium, represents the excess cost investors are paying for options protection above the turbulence actually being experienced.

In the options market, the evidence is unambiguous: the SPX put/call ratio reached 1.21 as of March 27, well above the neutral threshold of 1.0, indicating that demand for downside protection is overwhelming call activity. SPY's 30-day put/call ratio by open interest sits even higher at 1.41.

The options market is buying insurance for a level of destruction that has not yet arrived.

Three Fears, Partially Priced

The anxiety is not baseless. It rests on three identifiable pillars:

  1. The energy shock. WTI crude settled at $106, its highest close since July 2022. But $106 is not the intra-month spike toward $120 that initially triggered the panic. The first wave of fear has partially receded; the risk premium has not.

  2. Sticky inflation, patient Fed. The Fed held rates at the March meeting. On Monday, Chair Powell reinforced the stance at Harvard, stating that the tendency is to "look through" supply shocks rather than tighten into them. Translation: no near-term cuts, but no hikes either.

  3. A deteriorating labor print. February's payroll report showed a loss of 92,000 jobs, introducing recession risk into a market that was still debating the timing of rate cuts just weeks ago.

Each concern is legitimate. Together they form a coherent bear case. The question for positioning is how much is already reflected in prices.

Damage Check: Fear vs. Fundamentals

Despite the extreme sentiment readings, the S&P 500 has declined roughly 9% from its highs. The Nasdaq has entered correction territory at approximately 13% below its October record. These are meaningful drawdowns.

Meanwhile, the positioning data has become historically one-sided:

  • Morningstar's composite valuation model puts the U.S. equity market at a 12% discount to fair value. The technology sector trades at a 23% discount, its deepest since the 2022 bottom.

Morningstar Price/Fair Value by Sector

  • Median short interest in Russell 3000 stocks has climbed to 4.3%, a 15-year high and a full percentage point above the 2022 bear market peak. Energy sector short interest has doubled in weeks to its highest level since 2008.

  • The CNN Fear & Greed Index at 9 is approaching the levels last seen during the pandemic selloff.

When fear becomes this crowded, the historical pattern tends to favor the contrarian. That pattern is never guaranteed, but the asymmetry in positioning is hard to ignore.

What Resolves the Gap?

The implied-versus-realized volatility spread closes in one of two directions:

  • Fear fades. Implied vol normalizes downward. This requires credible de-escalation signals from the Middle East and benign inflation data.

  • The storm arrives. Realized vol catches up. This likely requires a fresh escalation (a prolonged Hormuz blockade, full-scale strikes on regional oil infrastructure) or a sharply negative NFP print that shifts consensus firmly toward recession.

Near-term catalysts to watch:

  1. April ECB meeting and the next U.S. PPI release for inflation trajectory

  2. March non-farm payrolls, scheduled for Good Friday morning, April 3, with U.S. markets closed

  3. Q1 earnings season beginning the week of April 13, which will test whether managements embed the pessimism into forward guidance or push back against it

The Takeaway

The market is paying an unusually high premium for insurance against a worst case that remains a scenario, not yet a reality. That does not make the fears wrong. It makes them expensive.

For investors with a longer time horizon, the dislocation between sentiment and fundamentals is worth close attention. The risk is not being fearful.

The risk is becoming fearful at the exact moment everyone else already is.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: Bitcoin Snaps 5-Month Losing Streak: Institutional Inflows And Trendline Break Fuel $80k Outlook
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has kickstarted the new quarter with a renewed sense of optimism, snapping a grueling five-month losing streak, its longest since 2018. After a bearish start to the week, the premier cryptocurrency has caught a bid in early Wednesday trade, reclaiming the $68,500 handle and teasing a breakout toward psychological resistance at $70,000.

The shift in sentiment is palpable as a combination of institutional re-engagement and a sudden de-escalation in Middle East tensions provides the "risk-on" spark that bulls have been waiting for.

Early Trade: Macro Tailwinds and the "Trump Reversal"

The early move today saw Bitcoin briefly touch $69,300, buoyed by headlines suggesting a diplomatic path forward in the US-Iran conflict. President Trump’s recent signals regarding a limited four-to-six-week military timeline, coupled with conciliatory remarks from Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, have allowed markets to price out some of the "war premium" that has weighed on risk assets.

From a fundamental perspective, the "dry spell" in institutional demand appears to be ending. Bitcoin spot ETFs recorded over $117 million in inflows on Tuesday, the second consecutive day of positive growth.

More importantly, March concluded with total ETF inflows of $1.32 billion, effectively ending a four-month streak of net withdrawals. This may be seen as a suggestion that the "smart money" is beginning to view the sub-$70k levels as an attractive entry point for Q2.

The "Strategy" Factor: Saylor’s Accumulation Engine Restarts

A significant driver for this week’s price action is the resurgence of Michael Saylor’s "Strategy" (MSTR) as a primary buyer. With the company's STRC preferred stock trading back above its $100 par value, the window for capital raising has reopened.

Estimates suggest Strategy is positioned to acquire over 1,100 BTC (~$76.25 million) this week alone. Historically, Bitcoin has shown a strong correlation with MSTR’s buying cycles, often rallying significantly when the company’s accumulation engine is in full gear.

The Road Ahead: Momentum vs. History

While the "hopium" is high, we must remain objective. Historical data from CoinGlass shows that while April is typically a "green" month (averaging 12.2% returns), Bitcoin has a habit of reversing its March trend. Since March closed slightly in the green, contrarians might argue for a cautious outlook.

However, if history repeats the 2018/2019 cycle where breaking a multi-month losing streak led to a 300% rally, the current consolidation might just be the launchpad for a historic Q2.

Technical Analysis: A Squeeze Toward $80,000?

Looking at the charts, Bitcoin’s recent bounce from the $60,000 floor, which many now view as a local bottom, is technically significant. The pair has successfully retested the lower boundary of a prevailing bear flag pattern and held.

Despite the retreat below $70,000, the technical outlook remains cautiously optimistic rather than bearish. The trendline break on the H4 chart below also reinforces this idea of a move to the upside.

I will be watching the $71,000 level closely; a break here confirms that the bulls are back in the driver's seat with $80,000 firmly in their crosshairs.

Key Levels to Watch:

  • Resistance: The immediate hurdle sits in the $69,300 - $71,000 zone. This area is congested with the 50-day EMA and a massive supply zone where roughly 650,000 BTC were previously acquired. A sustained daily close above $72,600 would be the "smoking gun" for bulls, likely triggering a fast move toward $80,000.
  • Support: On the downside, the $65,900 level remains pivotal. Should we lose this, the 200-week SMA near $59,400 would be the final line of defense before a deeper correction toward the $50,000 psychological mark.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) Four-Hour Chart, April 1, 2026

Original Post

data.title: Renasant Is Worth Watching In 2026
data.description:
This article serves as an update from my last analysis of Renasant Corporation (RNST) that was published almost four months ago on Seeking Alpha. I rated its stock as a Hold as I did in a previous review from August of last year. My opinion of RNST seems to have been appropriate so far. The stock has gained 3.4% when dividends are included since that August analysis was posted online. The S&P 500 has returned just over 4% during this same time.

Last year was a significant one for Renasant as it acquired the operations of The First Bancshares at the end of Q1 2025. The $1.2 billion deal gave RNST even deeper penetration into the Southeast, making it a larger competitor for companies like Regions Financial (RF), Pinnacle Financial (PNFP), and SouthState Bank (SSB). As of April 1, the market cap for Renasant Corporation stood at $3.28 billion.

Even though its assets made a big leap forward in 2025 thanks to the aforementioned acquisition, RNST’s earnings growth had been somewhat muted or even nonexistent in recent years, which was a large reason for my hesitation to consider the stock a Buy. However, FY 2025 ended on a high note for Renasant as its Q4 adjusted earnings of $0.91 per share beat expectations by $0.11. The company also made some other notable improvements in its operations, giving me much more reason to keep an eye on its Q1 and Q2 results from this year. I am maintaining my Hold rating at this current time, but I can see improvements in the company’s results that should carry over into this current year.

Company Overview

Mississippi-based Renasant Corporation is the holding company for Renasant Bank and has a history that dates back over 120 years. The company controls over 280 banking, mortgage, and wealth management offices in eight Southeastern states. The largest share of Renasant’s operations is concentrated in Mississippi and Georgia. The company also owns Continental Republic Capital, LLC, a finance subsidiary that specializes in factoring and commercial lending that operates under the name Republic Business Credit. An overview of Renasant’s physical office locations as well as the geographic diversity of the bank's loans and deposits is shown below.

Graphs of some key quarterly balance sheet figures from Renasant through the end of last year show another view of the company’s operations. This also shows the significance of the addition of The First Bancshares in the start of Q2. Total assets for RNST stood at $26.8 billion on the final day of 2025, 48.3% more than at the end of the previous year. Shareholders’ equity equaled $3.88 billion at the end of Q4 2025, 45.1% higher than 12 months prior.

Further details about the loans held by Renasant at the end of 2025 are shown below along with their progress from the previous quarter. Commercial real estate makes up just over 50% of the total portfolio with about two-thirds of that amount allocated to investor CRE. The bank’s exposure to the housing market is also significant as 24.3% of Renasant’s loan mix can be found in residential mortgages.

Renasant Corporation has a forward yield of 2.60%, about 48 basis points below the mean for the financial sector. Its quarterly dividend of $0.23 was raised last October from its previous $0.22. This increase was the first for the company since Q2 2019. Analysts are expecting another announcement later this year that its quarterly payout will tick upward again, albeit by just $0.01, as it did in the second half of 2025.

Analysis of Renasant Corporation

As I mentioned in my introduction, Q4 2025 was particularly strong for Renasant Corporation and a big reason behind much of my newfound optimism for the company. The non-GAAP profit of $0.91 per share was 18.2% better than Q3 2025 and 24.7% ahead of the earnings reported for Q4 2024. For FY 2025, RNST booked adjusted earnings of $3.06 per share, 10.9% ahead of its adjusted EPS of $2.76 from FY 2024.

When I last wrote about Renasant, FY 2025 EPS were projected to be $2.94, so the additional $0.12 was a good sign that the company had its operations moving in the right direction. Earnings estimates for FY 2026 now stand at $3.57 per share after being just $3.49 in early December. Earnings growth of 16.7% for this year is nothing spectacular, but it should be welcomed by shareholders who have invested in Renasant over the last several years when the company endured no earnings growth from 2021 through 2024.

The company’s expansion of its bottom line and its opportunities for a continuation of that trend are based in large part on the improvement made in its profitability metrics over the last year. Return on assets for the bank stood at 1.29%, 26 basis points higher than at the end of the previous year. Return on equity, a ratio that I normally like to see above 10%, was at 8.14% at the end of 2025. However, this was noticeably better than the 6.70% reported for Q4 2024. Net interest margin in Q4 of last year finished at 3.89% after starting the year at 3.36%. Renasant Bank, like most other regional banks in the US, was able to benefit from a significant reduction in its cost of deposits last year. RNST saw this number fall under 2% to 1.97% in Q4.

At first glance, asset quality for Renasant Bank appears to be relatively strong. Past due loans did show a noticeable uptick in Q4 from 0.26% in Q3 to 0.47%. However, criticized loans fell 28 basis points from the previous quarter to finish the year at 2.94%. The company stated that 80% of its criticized loan payments were less than 30 days past due at the end of Q4 2025.

From a valuation standpoint, the picture for Renasant is one of “good but not great." Its stock currently trades at a forward PE of 10.1, which is right around the median for its sector. The book value per share of $41.05 was 2.6% lower than where it stood at the end of 2024, showing the impact of the transaction with The First Bancshares. However, even with this decline, RNST currently trades at a price to book multiple of 0.89. The catch here is the disparity between book value and tangible book value, the latter of which I believe is often more valuable in evaluating regional banks. Renasant has a TBV of just $24.65 per share, so its price to TBV ratio is 1.49, just above the most recent national average, according to S&P Global.

Risks to Consider

There are a few additional aspects of the asset quality for Renasant Corporation that are worth watching in the first half of this year. The bank’s ratios for non-performing loans and non-performing assets have been trending gradually higher since Q2 2025. The total value for non-performing loans has been increasing as well. The largest share of issues seems to lie in the company’s home mortgage loans, but RNST also has $74.7 million in non-performing CRE loans on its books as well.

Some of the biggest risks involved with an investment in Renasant Corporation involve its relatively small size and limited geographic footprint. A pullback in the overall US economy could have a negative impact on loan and deposit growth at Renasant Bank. RNST does not have the same cushion of assets like some of the larger players in the Southeast mentioned earlier, like RF, PNFP, and SSB. In the event of a economic contraction, investors in the financial space may engage in a flight to safety that supports buying larger, more well-known names in the sector.

As I stated in previous articles, I believe the Southeast is a great area for regional banks to thrive, but it is not immune to some of the headwinds facing all areas of the US. Rising fuel prices, lagging job growth, and higher interest rates have caused a recalibration of expectations for the first half of this year. Renasant Bank’s commercial loans are dependent on the profitability of local businesses and the overall health of the consumers in their area. Growth in home mortgages, which make up almost a quarter of the company’s total gross loans, depends largely on home-buying activity, which has been held back by mortgage loan rates, which have been rising in recent weeks.

The Bottom Line

When I last wrote about Renasant Corporation, Seeking Alpha’s quant rating for the company was a Sell. As of April 1, that assessment has jumped up to a Buy, recognizing the improvement in the outlook for the company. Other analysts have become more optimistic for RNST as well. The company has received six EPS upward revisions in the last three months. There are only seven Wall Street analysts total that cover the company.

Renasant’s most recent quarter showed strong improvements since my first two articles about the company were published on Seeking Alpha. I will be looking to see if the company can build off its earnings momentum in Q1 2026 when those results are released on April 21. I also think that further consolidation for regional banks in the Southeast is likely later this year as companies may want to take full advantage of the current regulatory environment before potential changes in Congress follow from the upcoming midterm elections. My Hold rating for RNST is much closer to Buy now than it was during my last two analyses.
